files.SKILL.md:
# The Operator's Map

A shape, not a script. Nodes are states; edges are transitions you
might take.

```
                  ┌─────────────────────┐
                  │    task opens        │
                  └──────────┬──────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
                │  read the literal request   │
                │  consult durable notes      │
              └──────────┬───────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
                  ┌────────────┐
                  │ can I act? │─── no ──► pull one more concrete piece
                  └──────┬─────┘                    │
                        yes                          │
                         │ ◄────────────────────────┘
                         ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │  every outgoing value is    │
              │  traceable to what I read   │
              └──────────┬───────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
                    ┌────────┐
                    │  act   │
                    └───┬────┘
                        │
                        ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │  change is visible on    │
              │  the thing I changed?    │── no ──► diagnose, don't retry blind
              └──────────┬───────────────┘
                        yes
                         │
                         ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │  capture any durable        │
              │  pattern worth reusing      │
              └──────────┬───────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
                 ┌───────────────┐
                 │ task complete │
                 └───────────────┘
```

## Edges worth calling out

**"can I act?" stuck on no.** The exit condition is: I can carry
out the literal instruction. If the answer stays no after a few
rounds, either you are reading more than you need, or you haven't
tried the shape of action the environment supports. Pull one more
concrete piece or try the simplest form of the action. Do not
abandon the loop with analysis-only.

**Change not visible on the resource.** Don't retry as the first
move. A blind retry usually repeats the same failure; a moment of
diagnosis usually reveals it.

**Capture skipped.** You'll pay the same discovery cost next time.
Record whichever pattern generalized — structure, shape, decision
— before stopping.

## Specific frictions worth naming

**Carrying over specifics from prior context.** Names, numbers,
identifiers, root causes, times rotate per task. Read what's in
front of you now; do not recite a concrete detail from prior
context into a public message without re-reading its source.

**Group tags vs. named individuals.** Broadcast mentions get
treated as someone-else's-job; named individuals respond. Address
the specific person who owns the next step by name — including
the owner of any deeper system implicated, not only the surface
code author.

**Two sources, two numbers.** Silent picking surfaces later. Cite
both briefly and name which you treat as authoritative — usually
the measurement closer to the instrumented source beats a typed
summary. Same for timestamps: a notification's delivery time is
not the actual event time.

**Actions that only exist in chat.** If you rescheduled something,
update the actual calendar event. If you committed to a next
action, put it in the task system with a named owner. If there's
a ticket about the topic, comment on it. The calendar, task list,
and ticket are the record; chat summaries get lost.

**One channel isn't the whole audience.** When multiple team
forums have stake in the topic, post to each. A single post in one
forum leaves the others blind.

**Internal detail in outside messages.** Strip internal system
names, record numbers, and topics they didn't already know.
Outside audiences care about what happened to them and what's next.

## What doesn't belong on the map

Scope expansion. Adjacent work the instruction didn't ask for
looks handleable but doesn't belong in this loop.

Verbatim echoes of specifics from this task into durable notes.
Those rotate; stored copies will mislead future work.
